title: Median of Two Sorted Arrays
description:
Problem Description

Given two sorted arrays nums1 and nums2 of size m and n respectively, return the median of the two sorted arrays.

The overall run time complexity should be O(log (m+n)).

What is a Median?

The median is the middle value of a dataset. If the dataset has an odd number of observations, the middle one is the median. If the dataset has an even number of observations, the median is the average of the two middle values.

Examples

Example 1:

nums1 = [1, 3]
nums2 = [2]

The median is 2.0

Explanation: When we merge the two arrays, we get [1, 2, 3]. The median of this merged array is 2 (the middle element).

Example 2:

nums1 = [1, 2]
nums2 = [3, 4]

The median is 2.5

Explanation: When we merge the two arrays, we get [1, 2, 3, 4]. The median is (2 + 3) / 2 = 2.5 (the average of the two middle elements).

Example 3:

nums1 = [0, 0]
nums2 = [0, 0]

The median is 0.0

Explanation: The merged array is [0, 0, 0, 0] and the median is 0.

Example 4:

nums1 = []
nums2 = [1]

The median is 1.0

Explanation: The merged array has only one element, so the median is that element.

Constraints

  • nums1.length == m
  • nums2.length == n
  • 0 ≤ m ≤ 1000
  • 0 ≤ n ≤ 1000
  • 1 ≤ m + n ≤ 2000
  • -10^6 ≤ nums1[i], nums2[i] ≤ 10^6

Approach Hints

  1. Naive Approach: Merge the two arrays and find the median. This approach has O(m+n) time complexity, which doesn't meet our requirement.

  2. Binary Search Approach: We can use binary search to find the correct partition of the two arrays such that:

    • Left parts of both arrays have exactly (m+n+1)/2 elements
    • Every element in the left parts is ≤ every element in the right parts

    This approach has O(log(min(m,n))) time complexity.

Edge Cases to Consider

  • One or both arrays might be empty
  • Arrays might have different lengths
  • Arrays might have duplicate elements
  • The total number of elements might be odd or even

Implementation Considerations

When implementing your solution, be careful with:

  1. Index out of bounds errors
  2. Integer division vs. floating-point division
  3. Edge cases when partitioning the arrays
  4. Handling cases where all elements from one array are less than all elements from the other array

This problem is considered hard because it combines binary search with the concept of finding a median, and requires careful handling of edge cases.
